summary: Dental abscesses are acute bacterial infections requiring urgent intervention. Periapical abscesses originate from pulp necrosis; periodontal abscesses from deep periodontal pockets. Treatment involves establishing drainage (incision or through root canal access), antibiotic therapy for systemic involvement, and definitive treatment of the underlying cause. Untreated abscesses can lead to cellulitis, Ludwig's angina, or mediastinitis — potentially life-threatening complications.
risk_factors: Delayed treatment allowing spread to fascial spaces
Proximity to vital structures (airway, cavernous sinus)
alternatives: Incision and drainage with culture and sensitivity testing
Root canal therapy (if tooth is restorable)
Extraction with socket debridement (if tooth is non-restorable)
Hospital admission with IV antibiotics (for severe fascial space infections)
referral_summary: Urgent referral to oral surgeon or emergency department for: trismus (limited mouth opening), dysphagia (difficulty swallowing), fever >101°F, rapid spread of swelling, or immunocompromised patients. Refer to endodontist for complex root canal therapy after acute phase.
patient_education: A dental abscess is a serious infection — a collection of pus caused by bacteria. It needs treatment right away to prevent the infection from spreading. Treatment usually involves draining the infection and taking antibiotics. Once the acute infection is controlled, we'll treat the underlying problem — either a root canal to save the tooth or extraction if the tooth can't be saved. Warning signs to go to the ER: difficulty breathing or swallowing, fever, or swelling spreading to your eye or neck.
